_purpose: Structured handoff from the industry landscape report to org-level reports. This file contains the key sector-level findings that org reports reference. Generated from industry_canonical_cmhc_v3.json + the editorial analysis in FL_CMHC_Landscape_Report_v4.html.
_usage: Pass this file alongside org_canonical_input.json to the LLM when generating org reports. The LLM uses these numbers to position each org within the sector story.
_benchmark_philosophy: The CMHC sector is not the benchmark. Rest-of-FL P75 is the transformation target. CMHC P50 is context only. Rest-of-FL excludes CMHCs from the comparison pool (same as the industry report).

data_calibration._source: Raw DOGE (medicaid-provider-spending) joined to FL Provider Master List (stg_pml, 224K NPIs). No BH filters. External: KFF, AHCA, DCF, SAMHSA, MACPAC, Centene. Computed 2026-04-06.
data_calibration._method: FL providers identified via stg_pml (program_state='FL'). CMHC = taxonomy 261QM0801X (86 orgs). All denominators use full PML, not BH-filtered NPIs.

doge_universe.note: DOGE includes FFS + partial MCO encounter data. Excludes: inpatient (DRGs), pharmacy, LTSS capitation, nursing facility per-diem.

calibration_table.label: Outpatient BH
calibration_table.rationale: BH H-codes are purpose-built for behavioral health. High FFS capture. MCO encounter data partially flows through.

children.label: Intake / Assessment
children.rationale: BH-specific codes. Near-complete capture. CMHCs see 16% of people but collect 11% of dollars — lower payment per visit.

children.label: High Acuity / ACT
children.rationale: Specialized crisis/ACT. CMHCs serve 22% of crisis patients but capture only 15.5% of dollars.

children.label: Psychosocial Rehab
children.rationale: Single BH-specific code. Largest OP BH category. Dollar and bene shares align.

children.label: Therapy & Counseling
children.rationale: H2019 well-captured. CPT 908xx heavily billed by independent LMHCs/LCSWs. CMHCs serve 9.6% of therapy patients.

children.label: Care Management
children.rationale: BH-heavy but also used in disability/LTSS. CMHCs manage 12.4% of beneficiaries.

children.label: Community Support
children.rationale: Non-CMHC community orgs dominate. CMHCs essentially absent.

children.label: Medication Management
children.rationale: Small but CMHC-concentrated. Highest CMHC dollar share.

calibration_table.label: Substance Use Disorder (SUD)
calibration_table.rationale: Almost entirely invisible. Residential detox = revenue codes (not in DOGE). MAT = pharmacy ($90-130M, not in DOGE). Only H0020 methadone dosing ($12M) visible. FL ranks 51st of 52 in per-capita MH spending.
published_refs: DCF safety-net SUD budget: $482M (FY2024-25, NOT Medicaid)
Only 28% of FL Medicaid adults with OUD initiate MAT

calibration_table.label: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA)
calibration_table.rationale: Legislature budgeted $1.52B (FY24-25). SSEC revised to $1.63B for 8 months. Centene est ~$2.6B annualized. ABA was FFS until Feb 2025 then moved to SMMC 3.0. Massive cell suppression (individual child billing). Enrollment moratorium in Miami-Dade/Broward. National Medicaid ABA = $14.5B (279% growth since 2018).

calibration_table.label: Inpatient Behavioral Health
calibration_table.rationale: Completely invisible — DRG/revenue code billing excluded from DOGE. FL has ~174K Baker Act involuntary exams/yr, ~50% Medicaid. 1115 IMD waiver pending (up to 90-day stays, effective Jul 2026). MACPAC: FL FFS hospital total = $2.72B (all hospital, not just psych).

calibration_table.label: E&M (Office Visits)
calibration_table.rationale: Mostly non-BH (PCP, specialist). BH portion est $50-80M. CMHCs barely participate (0.6%).

calibration_table.label: Pharmacy (Psychotropic)
calibration_table.rationale: No Rx data in DOGE. Antipsychotics $420-480M (Invega Sustenna alone $120-140M). Antidepressants $78-90M. ADHD $60-100M. MAT/SUD meds $90-130M. FL total Medicaid pharmacy est $2.0-2.6B; psychotropics = 30-35%.

what_doge_misses: Inpatient facility claims (DRGs, revenue codes) — est. $500M-$1B BH portion
MCO-routed services (ABA $1.5-2.6B, SUD bulk) — capitation bundles
Claims with <12 encounters (cell suppression — significant for ABA)

market_composition._source: hcpcs_rate_benchmarks (BQ) + stg_doge confirmed FBHA NPIs. Recomputed 2026-04-06.
market_composition._fbha_coverage: 49/64 FBHA orgs with billing data, 86 matched NPIs, $60.5M of $65.3M benchmark total

intake.label: Intake & Assessment
intake.insight: Small market ($14M), but CMHCs own the gateway — 15% capture

high_acuity.label: High Acuity / ACT
high_acuity.insight: Small market but CMHC-dominated — 32% capture across ACT/FACT and community psych

ongoing_bh.label: Ongoing BH Services
ongoing_bh.insight: Majority of BH spend — PSR alone is $173M. CMHC anchor at $53M revenue

psr.label: Psychosocial Rehab
psr.insight: Single largest BH code — $173M market, CMHCs capture 10%

therapy.label: Therapy & Counseling
therapy.insight: H2019 is CMHC anchor ($13.8M); CPT therapy codes underutilized

care_mgmt.label: Care Management
care_mgmt.insight: T1017+T1015 drive $18M — second largest CMHC revenue category

eval_mgmt.label: E&M (Eval & Mgmt)
eval_mgmt.insight: Largest overall market ($233M) but CMHCs barely participate — 1% capture

med_mgmt.label: Medication Mgmt
med_mgmt.insight: CMHCs punch above weight — 25% capture in a $19M market

problem_1_rates.label: Lower Effective Rates
problem_1_rates.description: CMHCs collect less per beneficiary than non-CMHC providers on intake and high-acuity services. The net rate loss across the sector's current patient volume is $8.1M.

ongoing.note: Surprising finding — CMHCs earn more per bene in ongoing care. May reflect selection effect, code mix, or engagement depth.

problem_1_rates.sector_context_for_org: Performing 'at the CMHC median' may still represent underperformance relative to the broader market. The aspirational benchmark is closing the gap to the All FL Medicaid median.

problem_2_engagement.label: Lack of Patient Engagement (Leakage)
problem_2_engagement.description: CMHCs capture 15% of FL Medicaid BH intake but only 11.8% of ongoing care. The leakage gap is smaller than initially estimated (~$14M) but compounds with rate gaps. High acuity capture (32%) shows CMHCs are the crisis backbone — the question is whether patients stay after stabilization.

leakage_math.note: Intake capture (15%) vs ongoing capture (11.8%) — gap is smaller than previously estimated but still meaningful at $14M.

surprising_finding.implication: The sector doesn't have an ongoing care rate problem — it has an ongoing care volume problem. When patients stay, CMHCs deliver more intensive, higher-value care.

problem_2_engagement.sector_context_for_org: CMHCs already retain better than previously estimated (11.8% vs 15% intake share). But the real leakage signal is in high acuity: CMHCs handle 32% of crisis/ACT but only 12% of ongoing BH. The question is post-crisis retention — do patients step down to ongoing CMHC care or leave the system?

problem_3_burnout.label: Caseload Imbalance & Clinician Burnout
problem_3_burnout.description: An outcome of Problems 1 and 2. Lower rates force higher volume. Lower engagement means constant new-patient acquisition. Both compress clinician capacity and create a lopsided caseload — intake clinicians are overloaded while ongoing care clinicians are underutilized.

caseload_kpi.headline: Intake clinicians carry 50% more patients than industry. Ongoing care clinicians serve 46% fewer.

intake_overload.label: Intake Assessment
intake_overload.interpretation: CMHC intake clinicians process 50% more patients than the market — constant new-patient churn from leakage forces this throughput

ongoing_underload.label: Individual Therapy
ongoing_underload.interpretation: Therapy panels are half the market size — patients leave before building sustained caseloads

care_mgmt_load.label: Care Management
care_mgmt_load.interpretation: Already at industry level, but these clinicians carry the coordination burden for the entire patient flow

caseload_kpi.imbalance_label: 3.3x more intake volume per clinician than therapy volume
caseload_kpi.imbalance_note: In a balanced system, intake and therapy panels would grow proportionally. The 3.3x ratio shows the front door is overwhelmed while the back door leaks.

panel_gaps.note: Half the therapy panel of the market

burnout_cycle: Low rates → clinicians must see more patients to generate revenue
Low engagement → patients leave, forcing constant new-patient acquisition
High intake throughput → front-door clinicians stretched thin
Burnout → turnover → capacity loss → revenue loss
problem_3_burnout.sector_context_for_org: Each org feels this cycle differently based on their rate position (Problem 1) and engagement pattern (Problem 2). Orgs with above-market rates have a buffer. Orgs with strong retention have less churn pressure.

problem_4_it_spend.label: High Spend on IT
problem_4_it_spend.note: To be analyzed in a future iteration. Placeholder for IT spend analysis.

rest_of_fl_benchmarks._note: Rest-of-FL excludes CMHCs. P75 is the transformation target. These are the numbers org reports benchmark against.

published_rate_comparison._note: Compares CMHC effective rates (P50 payment-per-claim) against FL Medicaid published fee schedule rates (AHCA Rule 59G-4.002). Gap shows how much CMHCs collect vs what the state officially pays.

H2019.note: Individual/family therapy — bundled claim, not comparable to per-unit published rate

H2017.note: Psychosocial rehab — bundled claim, not comparable to per-unit published rate

T1017.note: Care management — bundled claim; CMHCs below market on effective rate

T1015.note: Med mgmt — CMHCs collect LESS than published rate

H0031.note: Tiered by modifier: limited $17.90, bio-psychosocial $57.28, in-depth new $126.11

H0040.note: FACT per diem — near published rate

99214.note: E&M Estab Pt Lv4 — near published rate, CMHCs outperform market

90791.note: Psychiatric diagnostic eval — CMHCs above market but below published

90792.note: Psychiatric eval w/ med — CMHCs well below market and published

H0036.note: Community psych support — CMHCs dominate claims but collect 28% less than market

H2010.note: Comprehensive med services — bundled claim, CMHCs below market

90832.note: Psychotherapy 30 min — CMHCs above market but negligible volume

90834.note: Psychotherapy 45 min — CMHCs below both published and market

90837.note: Psychotherapy 60 min — CMHCs 33% below market, significant volume

H0004.note: BH counseling — CMHCs effectively not collecting on this code

H0048.note: Alcohol/drug screening — all providers near published rate

T1007.note: Treatment plan review — CMHCs well above market and published

99202.note: E&M New Pt Lv2 — CMHCs do not bill this code

99203.note: E&M New Pt Lv3 — negligible CMHC volume

99204.note: E&M New Pt Lv4 — negligible CMHC volume

99205.note: E&M New Pt Lv5 — CMHCs do not bill this code

99212.note: E&M Estab Pt Lv2 — CMHCs 24% below published

99213.note: E&M Estab Pt Lv3 — CMHCs 35% below published

99215.note: E&M Estab Pt Lv5 — CMHCs above market, near published

90833.note: Psychotherapy add-on 30 min — CMHCs above both market and published

90836.note: Psychotherapy add-on 45 min — CMHCs do not bill this code

90838.note: Psychotherapy add-on 60 min — CMHCs do not bill this code

key_findings: Time-based codes (H2019, H2017, T1017) have per-15min published rates but are billed as bundled claims — direct ratio comparison is not meaningful
Event-based codes (T1015, H0031, H0032, H2000) can be compared directly: effective rates are AT or BELOW published — these are the true rate pressure points
H0031 (intake assessment) collects only 25% of published rate — most CMHCs bill the 'limited' version ($17.90) rather than the 'in-depth' ($126.11)
T1015 (med mgmt) is the only high-volume code where CMHCs collect less than the published rate — a direct margin squeeze

fqhc_comparison._note: CMHC vs FQHC comparison across rates, leakage proxy, and productivity. FQHCs are federally funded primary care + BH sites operating under PPS — different funding model but competing for the same Medicaid BH patients.

H2019.note: CMHCs collect 70% more per therapy claim

T1015.note: FQHCs collect 2.7x more — PPS encounter rate vs fee-for-service

T1017.note: FQHCs do not typically bill care management

T1015.note: CMHCs handle more med mgmt patients per clinician

99214.note: FQHCs handle more E&M visits — primary care model

key_findings: CMHCs outperform FQHCs on BH-specific codes (therapy, PSR, intake) — their core competency
FQHCs dominate on medication management (T1015) due to PPS encounter-rate advantage — 2.7x higher effective rate
FQHCs do not compete in care management (T1017) — this is a CMHC-specific service line
CMHCs run leaner panels on E&M (100 vs 142 benes/clinician) but higher panels on therapy — reflects specialization
fqhc_comparison.strategic_implication: CMHCs should not try to out-FQHC the FQHCs on med mgmt. Their advantage is in BH-specific services where they collect 20-70% more per claim. The strategy is to deepen engagement in these services, not diversify into PPS-advantaged territory.

market_archetype_comparison._note: Comparison across FL market density tiers: dense (Miami-Dade, Broward, Palm Beach, Hillsborough, Orange, Duval), moderate (mid-size metros), sparse (rural). Market tier drives patient access, competition, and payer mix.
key_findings: Care management (T1017) shows the largest market-tier gap: dense-market providers collect 35% more per claim than sparse
Therapy rates (H2019) are surprisingly stable across markets — only 7% spread
Rural CMHCs face a double squeeze: lower rates AND smaller panels, especially in PSR and care management
E&M rates (99214) are highest in moderate markets — likely less price competition than dense metros

dense.label: Dense Metro
dense.profile: Higher rates, larger panels, more competition. Rate advantage from payer mix and negotiating leverage.
dense.risk: Leakage — patients have many provider options and churn easily

moderate.label: Mid-Size Metro
moderate.profile: Balanced rates, highest therapy panels. Sweet spot of patient access without extreme competition.
moderate.risk: Capacity — growing demand may outpace clinician supply

sparse.label: Rural / Sparse
sparse.profile: Lowest rates, smallest panels. Often the only BH provider in the area — monopoly position but thin margins.
sparse.risk: Sustainability — low rates + small panels make it hard to recruit and retain clinicians

headline: The rate problem is real. The productivity burden is real. Post-crisis retention is the hidden lever.

service_line_coverage._note: How many of the 86 FL CMHCs bill each code (2024). Source: hcpcs_rate_benchmarks, dimension=by_org_type, value=CMHC. Coverage % = org_count / 86. Codes with low coverage represent supply gaps — CMHCs don't offer the service, so patients go elsewhere.

intake.note: Only 56% of CMHCs bill intake assessment (H0031); eval codes have very low penetration

high_acuity.note: No high-acuity code is offered by a majority of CMHCs. H2017 (PSR) at 45% is the highest. ACT/FACT only 7%.

ongoing_bh.note: H2019 is universal (100%). But T1017 (care mgmt) only 40%. CPT psychotherapy codes (90832-90837) are offered by <31%.

eval_mgmt.note: 99214 at 76% is the anchor. Most other E&M codes under 10%.

med_mgmt.note: T1015 at 62% is reasonable. But 90833 (add-on psychotherapy during med visit) only 24%.

key_findings: Only 1 code (H2019) is billed by ALL 86 CMHCs — the sector has extremely narrow service offerings
High-acuity services have the lowest coverage: no code is offered by even half of CMHCs
Care management (T1017) — a $12.1M revenue line — is offered by only 34 of 86 CMHCs (40%)
CPT psychotherapy codes (90832-90837) are offered by <31% of CMHCs, explaining low ongoing BH market share
The leakage story is partly a SUPPLY story: patients can't stay for services that aren't offered

market_share_trends._source: hcpcs_rate_trends (BQ), CMHC vs all_fl by category. Recomputed 2026-04-06.
market_share_trends._note: CMHC market share (%) of BH-provider-filtered FL market by service category, annual.

caveats.beneficiary_deduplication: Market share percentages reflect the sector's share of beneficiaries within each service category, not tracked patient journeys. The true retention rate requires beneficiary-level linkage.
caveats.leakage_upper_bound: The $14.3M leakage estimate compares intake capture (15%) to ongoing capture (11.8%). True retention requires beneficiary-level linkage.
caveats.ongoing_rpb_advantage: The +48.7% ongoing RPB advantage may reflect selection effect, code mix, or engagement depth — not necessarily better reimbursement terms.
